questions.question: What happens to the brain during a stroke?
options: The brain becomes tired and needs a short rest to recover.
Only the muscles are affected, not the brain itself.
The blood supply to part of the brain is cut off, so brain cells are starved of oxygen and begin to die.
The brain produces too much blood and becomes overactive.

questions.question: A stroke is often called a "brain attack". What does the phrase "time is brain" mean?
options: The brain slows down time during a stroke.
Every minute of delay means more of the brain is lost.
A stroke only affects the brain at certain times of day.
There is plenty of time to watch and wait before acting.

questions.question: Why do the effects of a stroke differ from one person to another?
options: Because the effects depend only on how tall the person is.
Because they depend on which part of the brain is affected and how much damage is done.
Because effects are random and cannot be explained.
Because the effects depend on the person's age alone.

questions.question: Which is the most common type of stroke?
options: An ischaemic stroke.
A haemorrhagic stroke.
A transient ischaemic attack.
A stroke caused by tiredness.

questions.question: What causes an ischaemic stroke?
options: A blood vessel in the brain bursts and bleeds.
The person has not eaten enough that day.
A clot blocks a blood vessel supplying the brain.
The brain makes too much oxygen.

questions.question: What causes a haemorrhagic stroke?
options: A short, harmless interruption to blood flow.
The person's blood pressure drops too low.
A clot travels from the heart to the brain.
A blood vessel in or around the brain bursts and bleeds.

questions.question: Which best describes a TIA, or "mini-stroke"?
options: A stroke that lasts for several weeks.
A harmless event that never needs medical attention.
A brief interruption to the blood supply whose symptoms pass, but which is a serious warning sign.
A type of stroke that only affects children.

questions.question: How can medical staff tell which type of stroke a person has had?
options: By checking the person's temperature.
By waiting to see how quickly they recover.
By asking the person which type they think it is.
Only a hospital brain scan can tell them apart.

questions.question: Which is described as the single biggest risk factor for stroke?
options: Taking regular gentle walks.
High blood pressure, especially when it is not well controlled.
Reading in good lighting.
Drinking water with meals.

questions.question: Why does atrial fibrillation increase the risk of a stroke?
options: It has no real effect on stroke risk.
It lowers the person's blood pressure to a safe level.
This irregular heartbeat allows clots to form that can travel to the brain.
It makes the brain produce extra blood vessels.

questions.question: In the FAST test, what does the letter "T" stand for?
options: Test the person's balance.
Temperature to check.
Time to call 999.
Talk to the person calmly.

questions.question: When checking "Face" in the FAST test, what should you do?
options: Ask them to spell their name backwards.
Check whether their vision is blurred.
Ask them to raise both arms above their head.
Look at their face and ask them to smile, checking whether it has fallen on one side.

questions.question: When checking "Arms" in the FAST test, what are you looking for?
options: Whether their arms feel warm to the touch.
Whether they can write their name.
Whether they can raise both arms and keep them there, or whether one drifts downwards.
Whether they can clap their hands quickly.

questions.question: When checking "Speech" in the FAST test, what might you notice?
options: Their speech is slurred or muddled, or they cannot talk at all even though they seem awake.
The person is speaking more clearly than usual.
The person is humming a tune.
The person is speaking a foreign language.

questions.question: How many FAST signs must be present before you act?
options: At least three of the four signs.
All four signs must be present.
You must wait for the signs to appear twice.
Any single one of the signs is enough to act on.

questions.question: Besides the FAST signs, which sudden symptom could also be a sign of a stroke?
options: A sudden severe headache with no known cause, sometimes the worst the person has ever had.
A mild ache that has built up slowly over a week.
Feeling slightly hungry before lunch.
A gradual improvement in memory over several days.

questions.question: You notice a person has suddenly become muddled and one side of their mouth has drooped. What should you do?
options: Give them a hot drink and encourage them to rest.
Wait an hour to see whether they return to normal on their own.
Recognise this as a possible stroke, check FAST, and call 999 immediately.
Assume they are just tired and note it to mention next week.

questions.question: What is the single most important action when you suspect someone is having a stroke?
options: Help them lie down and check again later.
Phone the person's GP for an appointment.
Call 999 for an ambulance without delay and say you suspect a stroke.
Give the person their usual medication.

questions.question: A colleague suggests driving the person to hospital themselves. What should you do?
options: Call 999 so paramedics can begin assessment on the way and take them to the right stroke unit.
Ask the person which they would prefer.
Wait for a GP appointment instead.
Agree, as it will be faster than waiting.

questions.question: A person showed stroke signs a few minutes ago but now seems completely back to normal. What should you do?
options: Do nothing, because the signs have gone.
Ask them to lie down and check on them in a few hours.
Wait until tomorrow and mention it to the GP if it happens again.
Still call 999 — the signs may have been a TIA, a warning that a major stroke could follow.

questions.question: Why is it important to note the time the symptoms started?
options: Some stroke treatments can only be given within a set time from when the stroke began.
It helps decide whether to call 999 at all.
It is needed to fill in the person's care plan later.
It tells you which type of stroke the person has had.

questions.question: Why must you not give a person food or drink when you suspect a stroke?
options: Because eating slows down the recovery of brain cells.
A stroke can affect swallowing, so food or fluid could go into the airway and cause choking.
Because it would spoil their appetite for later.
Because the ambulance crew will bring their own food.

questions.question: After calling 999, what should you do while waiting for the ambulance?
options: Move them around to keep them active.
Give them a tablet to help with any pain.
Leave the person alone so they can rest quietly.
Stay with the person, keep them calm and comfortable, and watch them closely.

questions.question: What does the term "dysphagia" mean after a stroke?
options: A sudden severe headache.
Difficulty with vision in one eye.
Weakness down one side of the body.
Difficulty swallowing, which needs careful management to help prevent choking.

questions.question: Which statement about aphasia is correct?
options: It means the person's intelligence has been permanently damaged.
It makes it hard to speak, understand, read or write, but it does not affect the person's intelligence.
It only affects the person's ability to walk.
It is a temporary tiredness that passes within an hour.

questions.question: A stroke is a medical emergency that happens suddenly and is life-threatening.
options: True
False

questions.question: You should only call 999 if a person shows all four FAST signs at the same time.
options: True
False

questions.question: A TIA can safely be ignored if the person appears to recover quickly.
options: True
False

questions.question: High blood pressure is the single biggest risk factor for stroke.
options: True
False

questions.question: It is safe to give a person a small sip of water while waiting for the ambulance.
options: True
False

questions.question: Noting the time a person's stroke symptoms started is useful information for the hospital.
options: True
False

questions.question: Aphasia means a person's intelligence has been damaged by the stroke.
options: True
False

questions.question: Overwhelming fatigue after a stroke is a real effect and is not a sign of laziness.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of the following are recognised risk factors for stroke? (Select all that apply)
options: Reading in good lighting
Smoking
Drinking water with meals
Atrial fibrillation
High blood pressure

questions.question: Which of these are sudden signs of a stroke beyond the FAST test? (Select all that apply)
options: Sudden weakness or numbness down one side of the body
Sudden trouble with vision in one or both eyes
Sudden loss of balance or coordination
A gradual improvement in memory over a week
Slowly feeling hungrier than usual

questions.question: While waiting for the ambulance after calling 999, which actions are appropriate? (Select all that apply)
options: Give them a cup of tea to steady their nerves
Keep them calm and comfortable and reassure them
Note the time the symptoms started to pass to paramedics
Leave to phone the GP for advice
Stay with the person and do not leave them alone

questions.question: Which of the following must you NOT do when someone may be having a stroke? (Select all that apply)
options: Give them a drink
Give them food
Give them medication by mouth
Note the time the symptoms began
Call 999 for an ambulance

questions.question: Which of these are common effects of a stroke? (Select all that apply)
options: Overwhelming fatigue and emotional or cognitive changes
Weakness or paralysis down one side of the body
A permanent improvement in memory and concentration
Faster, stronger movement on one side
Difficulty swallowing (dysphagia)

questions.question: How can you best support someone with aphasia after a stroke? (Select all that apply)
options: Offer other ways to communicate, such as writing, pictures or gestures
Use short sentences and questions
Give them time and do not rush or finish their sentences
Talk loudly and quickly so they keep up
Speak only to their relatives and not to them

questions.question: Which everyday support can help reduce the risk of another stroke? (Select all that apply)
options: Encouraging the person to skip their medication
Supporting the person to take prescribed medicines as directed
Ignoring any new concerns rather than reporting them
Encouraging the person not to smoke
Encouraging a balanced diet and activity where it is in the care plan

questions.question: The FAST test stands for Face, Arms, Speech and ___.
An ischaemic stroke is caused by a ___ blocking the flow of blood to the brain.
A ___ stroke is caused by a blood vessel bursting and bleeding.
The moment you suspect someone is having a stroke, you must call ___ for an ambulance.
Difficulty swallowing after a stroke is known as ___.
